evals.name: scaffold-tlv
evals.prompt: I'm starting a small Go library for parsing simple TLV (type-length-value) records. Scaffold a new package called `tlv` so I can start filling in the format details.
evals.expected_output: A scaffolded Go package at ./tlv/ with doc.go, types.go, types_test.go, decoder.go, decoder_test.go, encoder.go, encoder_test.go, and CLAUDE.md. `go build` and `go test` should both succeed.

assertions.text: go test ./tlv/... exits cleanly with all tests passing
encoder.go defines `func Encode(w io.Writer, f *File) error`
decoder.go and encoder.go store byte order as `binary.ByteOrder` field
test files call t.Parallel() at both function and subtest level
tlv/CLAUDE.md documents byte order, error-wrapping convention, and testing style

evals.name: scaffold-gzip
evals.prompt: scaffold a new go binary file library package for the gzip format. call it gzip.
evals.expected_output: A scaffolded Go package at ./gzip/ with all 8 files. `go build` and `go test` should both succeed.

assertions.text: go test ./gzip/... exits cleanly with all tests passing
encoder.go defines `func Encode(w io.Writer, f *File) error`
decoder.go and encoder.go store byte order as `binary.ByteOrder` field
test files call t.Parallel() at both function and subtest level
gzip/CLAUDE.md documents byte order, error-wrapping convention, and testing style

evals.name: scaffold-dns
evals.prompt: I need to start a new Go package called `dns` for working with DNS wire-format messages. I'll be using the implement-binary-file-library agent afterward to fill in the real types from the RFC, but I want the boilerplate in place now.
evals.expected_output: A scaffolded Go package at ./dns/ with all 8 files. `go build` and `go test` should both succeed.

assertions.text: go test ./dns/... exits cleanly with all tests passing
encoder.go defines `func Encode(w io.Writer, f *File) error`
decoder.go and encoder.go store byte order as `binary.ByteOrder` field
test files call t.Parallel() at both function and subtest level
dns/CLAUDE.md documents byte order, error-wrapping convention, and testing style
